mnemonics.title: FILE OPENING MODES
mnemonics.content: READ: r (existing file) | WRITE: w (new/truncate) | APPEND: a (end of file) | PLUS: + (read+write) | BINARY: b (raw bytes). REMEMBER: R-W-A-P-B - READY TO WORK!

mnemonics.title: FILE CLOSING CHECK
mnemonics.content: ALWAYS CHECK: Always verify fopen() return value is not NULL before using the file pointer. NULL MEANS NO FILE.

mnemonics.title: TEXT VS BINARY
mnemonics.content: TEXT: t (translates special chars like \n) | BINARY: b (raw bytes, no translation). THINK: t = TRANSLATION, b = BYTES.

acronyms.usage: Used to detect when all data has been read from a file. Functions return EOF when end is reached.
A data structure in stdio.h that stores control information about an open file.
Function to open/create a file and return a FILE pointer.
Function to close an open file and flush buffers.
Default error output stream, unbuffered by default.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a file as a book and FILE pointer as a bookmark showing your current page position. Each read/write operation moves the bookmark forward.
Think of buffering like using a bucket to collect water before pouring. The bucket (buffer) collects small amounts of data, then dumps them all at once to the file (reducing trips).
A file stream is like a pipe connecting your program to the file. Data flows through this pipe - into the file (write) or out of the file (read).

File modes are like different doors: r is a door you can only enter (read), w is a door that clears the room before you enter (write/truncate), a is a door that takes you to the back of the room (append).

analogies.analogy: Variables are like sticky notes - they disappear when you close the program. Files are like notebooks - your notes stay even after you put the notebook away.

analogies.concept: fopen() as Opening a File Cabinet
analogies.analogy: fopen() is like requesting access to a specific drawer in a file cabinet. It gives you a handle (FILE pointer) to work with that drawer.
EOF is like an exit sign in a maze. When you reach it, you know there's nothing more to explore - you've reached the end of the file.
Different file modes are like different keys: 'r' is a read-only key, 'w' is a key that clears everything before writing, 'a' is a key that only works at the back of the file.
